---
title: "Lesson 5:  Properties of Maximum Likelihood Estimators"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_large-sample-properties-of-mles.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_large-sample-properties-of-mles.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-large-sample-properties-of-mles_module3_lesson5.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Slides on Large-Sample Properties of Maximum Likelihood Estimators {#sec-large-sample-properties-mles}

Let $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ be a random sample from a distribution with probability density function $f(x;\theta)$, and let $\widehat{\theta}_n$ be a maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) of $\theta$.

Under suitable regularity conditions, such as those required for the Cramér--Rao lower bound (CRLB), maximum likelihood estimators have the following large-sample properties.

::: {#thm-large-sample-properties-mles}
## Large-Sample Properties of MLEs

1. **Existence and uniqueness**

   The maximum likelihood estimator $\widehat{\theta}_n$ exists and is unique.

2. **Consistency**

   $$
   \widehat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta
   $$

   Thus, $\widehat{\theta}_n$ converges in probability to the true parameter $\theta$.

3. **Asymptotic unbiasedness**

   $$
   \lim_{n\to\infty}\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\theta}_n]=\theta
   $$

4. **Asymptotic efficiency**

   $$
   \lim_{n\to\infty}
   \frac{\mathrm{CRLB}_{\theta,n}}
        {\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\theta}_n]}
   =1
   $$

5. **Asymptotic normality**

   $$
   \widehat{\theta}_n
   \overset{\cdot}{\sim}
   \mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta,\mathrm{CRLB}_{\theta,n}\right)
   $$

   Equivalently,

   $$
   \frac{\widehat{\theta}_n-\theta}
        {\sqrt{\mathrm{CRLB}_{\theta,n}}}
   \xrightarrow{d}
   \mathcal{N}(0,1)
   $$
:::

## Exponential-Rate Example {#sec-exponential-rate-mle-example}

::: {#exm-exponential-rate-mle}
## Maximum Likelihood Estimation of an Exponential Rate

Let

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Exp}(\lambda)
$$

where $\lambda>0$ is the rate parameter. The maximum likelihood estimator of $\lambda$ is

$$
\widehat{\lambda}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}}
$$
:::

### Existence and Uniqueness {#sec-exponential-mle-existence-uniqueness}

The log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\lambda)
=
n\log(\lambda)-\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
$$

Its first and second derivatives are

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\ell(\lambda)}{d\lambda}
&=
\frac{n}{\lambda}-\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i \\
\frac{d^2\ell(\lambda)}{d\lambda^2}
&=
-\frac{n}{\lambda^2}<0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the unique maximizer is

$$
\widehat{\lambda}
=
\frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i}
=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}}
$$

### Asymptotic Unbiasedness {#sec-exponential-mle-asymptotic-unbiasedness}

Let

$$
Y=\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i
$$

Then

$$
Y\sim\operatorname{Gamma}(n,\lambda)
$$

where the second parameter is the rate. For $n>1$, since $\widehat{\lambda}=n/Y$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\lambda}]
&=
n\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{1}{Y}\right] \\
&=
n\left(\frac{\lambda}{n-1}\right) \\
&=
\frac{n}{n-1}\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus,

$$
\lim_{n\to\infty}\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\lambda}]
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{n-1}\lambda
=
\lambda
$$

Therefore, $\widehat{\lambda}$ is asymptotically unbiased.

## Probability Limits and the Continuous Mapping Theorem {#sec-probability-limits-continuous-mapping}

To establish consistency, we use the algebra of probability limits.

::: {#thm-algebra-probability-limits}
## Algebra of Probability Limits

Suppose that $\{X_n\}$ and $\{Y_n\}$ are sequences of random variables such that

$$
X_n\xrightarrow{P}X
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
Y_n\xrightarrow{P}Y
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_n+Y_n
&\xrightarrow{P}X+Y \\
X_nY_n
&\xrightarrow{P}XY \\
\frac{X_n}{Y_n}
&\xrightarrow{P}\frac{X}{Y}
&& \text{provided }\Pr(Y\neq0)=1
\end{aligned}
$$

Moreover, for every continuous function $g$,

$$
g(X_n)\xrightarrow{P}g(X)
$$
:::

### Consistency of the Exponential-Rate MLE {#sec-exponential-mle-consistency}

By the Weak Law of Large Numbers,

$$
\overline{X}
\xrightarrow{P}
\mathbb{E}[X_1]
=
\frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

The function $g(x)=1/x$ is continuous at $1/\lambda$. Therefore, by the continuous mapping theorem,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\widehat{\lambda}
&=
g(\overline{X}) \\
&=
\frac{1}{\overline{X}} \\
&\xrightarrow{P}
\frac{1}{1/\lambda} \\
&=
\color{#C62828}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $\widehat{\lambda}$ is consistent.

### Asymptotic Efficiency {#sec-exponential-mle-asymptotic-efficiency}

For the exponential-rate model, the Cramér--Rao lower bound is

$$
\mathrm{CRLB}_{\lambda,n}
=
\frac{\lambda^2}{n}
$$

To calculate $\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\lambda}]$, first calculate the second moment. For $n>2$, since $\widehat{\lambda}=n/Y$ and $Y\sim\operatorname{Gamma}(n,\lambda)$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\widehat{\lambda}^{\,2}\right]
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{n^2}{Y^2}\right] \\
&=
n^2\int_{0}^{\infty}
\frac{1}{y^2}
\frac{\lambda^n}{\Gamma(n)}
y^{n-1}e^{-\lambda y}\,dy \\
&=
n^2\int_{0}^{\infty}
\frac{\lambda^n}{\Gamma(n)}
y^{n-3}e^{-\lambda y}\,dy \\
&=
n^2\lambda^2
\frac{\Gamma(n-2)}{\Gamma(n)}
\underbrace{
\int_{0}^{\infty}
\frac{\lambda^{n-2}}{\Gamma(n-2)}
y^{n-3}e^{-\lambda y}\,dy
}_{\displaystyle =1\text{, the integral of a }\operatorname{Gamma}(n-2,\lambda)\text{ density}} \\
&=
n^2\lambda^2
\frac{\Gamma(n-2)}{\Gamma(n)} \\
&=
\frac{n^2}{(n-1)(n-2)}\lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\lambda}]
&=
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\widehat{\lambda}^{\,2}\right]
-
\left(\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\lambda}]\right)^2 \\
&=
\frac{n^2}{(n-1)(n-2)}\lambda^2
-
\left(\frac{n}{n-1}\lambda\right)^2 \\
&=
\frac{n^2}{(n-1)^2(n-2)}\lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The ratio of the Cramér--Rao lower bound to the variance is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\mathrm{CRLB}_{\lambda,n}}
     {\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\lambda}]}
&=
\frac{\lambda^2/n}
     {\dfrac{n^2}{(n-1)^2(n-2)}\lambda^2} \\
&=
\frac{(n-1)^2(n-2)}{n^3} \\
&\longrightarrow
1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $\widehat{\lambda}$ is asymptotically efficient.

## A General Consistency Criterion {#sec-general-consistency-criterion}

Recall that the Weak Law of Large Numbers establishes

$$
\overline{X}\xrightarrow{P}\mu
$$

using Chebyshev's inequality together with

$$
\mathbb{E}[\overline{X}]=\mu
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\operatorname{Var}[\overline{X}]\longrightarrow0
$$

The same argument gives a general consistency result.

::: {#thm-asymptotic-unbiasedness-vanishing-variance}
## Asymptotic Unbiasedness and Vanishing Variance Imply Consistency

Let $\widehat{\theta}_n$ be an estimator of $\theta$. If

$$
\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\theta}_n]\longrightarrow\theta
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\theta}_n]\longrightarrow0
$$

then

$$
\widehat{\theta}_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta
$$
:::

The result follows from the generalized Markov inequality because, for every $\varepsilon>0$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\!\left(
|\widehat{\theta}_n-\theta|\geq\varepsilon
\right)
&\leq
\frac{
\mathbb{E}\!\left[(\widehat{\theta}_n-\theta)^2\right]
}{\varepsilon^2} \\
&=
\frac{1}{\varepsilon^2}
\left[
\underbrace{
\operatorname{Var}[\widehat{\theta}_n]
}_{\displaystyle \longrightarrow 0}
+
\underbrace{
\left(\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\theta}_n]-\theta\right)^2
}_{\displaystyle \longrightarrow 0}
\right] \\
&\longrightarrow
0
\end{aligned}
$$

The unbiased case is obtained by setting $\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\theta}_n]=\theta$ for every $n$.

## Maximum of a Uniform Sample {#sec-maximum-uniform-sample}

::: {#exm-uniform-maximum-consistency}
## Maximum Likelihood Estimation for a Uniform Upper Bound

Let

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\operatorname{Unif}(0,\theta)
$$

The maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$ is the sample maximum

$$
Y_n
=
\max(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)
$$
:::

### Distribution of the Sample Maximum {#sec-distribution-sample-maximum}

For $0\leq y\leq\theta$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{Y_n}(y)
&=
\Pr(Y_n\leq y) \\
&=
\Pr\!\left(
\max(X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n)\leq y
\right) \\
&=
\Pr(X_1\leq y,X_2\leq y,\ldots,X_n\leq y) \\
&=
\Pr(X_1\leq y)\Pr(X_2\leq y)\cdots\Pr(X_n\leq y)
&& \text{by independence} \\
&=
\left[\Pr(X_1\leq y)\right]^n \\
&=
\left(\frac{y}{\theta}\right)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the cumulative distribution function is

$$
F_{Y_n}(y)
=
\begin{cases}
0, & y<0 \\
\left(\dfrac{y}{\theta}\right)^n, & 0\leq y\leq\theta \\
1, & y>\theta
\end{cases}
$$

Differentiating gives the probability density function

$$
f_{Y_n}(y)
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{n}{\theta^n}y^{n-1}, & 0\leq y\leq\theta \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Expected Value of the Sample Maximum {#sec-expectation-sample-maximum}

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[Y_n]
&=
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}y f_{Y_n}(y)\,dy \\
&=
\int_{0}^{\theta}
y\frac{n}{\theta^n}y^{n-1}\,dy \\
&=
\frac{n}{\theta^n}
\int_{0}^{\theta}y^n\,dy \\
&=
\frac{n}{\theta^n}
\left[\frac{y^{n+1}}{n+1}\right]_{0}^{\theta} \\
&=
\frac{n}{n+1}\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$
\mathbb{E}[Y_n]
=
\frac{n}{n+1}\theta
\longrightarrow
\theta
$$

so $Y_n$ is asymptotically unbiased.

### Variance of the Sample Maximum {#sec-variance-sample-maximum}

First,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[Y_n^2]
&=
\int_{0}^{\theta}
y^2\frac{n}{\theta^n}y^{n-1}\,dy \\
&=
\frac{n}{\theta^n}
\int_{0}^{\theta}y^{n+1}\,dy \\
&=
\frac{n}{n+2}\theta^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Var}[Y_n]
&=
\mathbb{E}[Y_n^2]
-
\left(\mathbb{E}[Y_n]\right)^2 \\
&=
\frac{n}{n+2}\theta^2
-
\left(\frac{n}{n+1}\theta\right)^2 \\
&=
\frac{n}{(n+1)^2(n+2)}\theta^2 \\
&\longrightarrow
0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Y_n$ is asymptotically unbiased and its variance converges to zero,

$$
\boxed{
\color{#C62828}{
Y_n\xrightarrow{P}\theta
}
}
$$

Therefore, the sample maximum is a consistent estimator of $\theta$.


## Reflection
